section.title: Walking by the Spirit
section.sectionRationale: Paul answers how the free person actually lives a holy life, not by law but by the Spirit. He sets the works of the flesh against the fruit of the Spirit, showing two opposed powers at war within. Those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh, and since they live by the Spirit they are to keep in step with the Spirit. Holiness is the harvest of walking with the Spirit, not the achievement of law keeping.
section.thesis: The believer walks in holiness not by law but by the Spirit, in whom the flesh and the Spirit war against each other; the works of the flesh are obvious and exclude from the kingdom, but the fruit of the Spirit grows in those who belong to Christ and have crucified the flesh, so they must keep in step with the Spirit.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the command to walk by the Spirit and the war between flesh and Spirit; the works of the flesh contrasted with the fruit of the Spirit; and the crucified flesh and the call to keep in step with the Spirit.

units.label: The war between flesh and Spirit
units.note: walk by the Spirit and you will not gratify the flesh

verses.text: So I say, walk by the {{pneuma|Spirit}}, and you will not gratify the desires of the {{sarx|flesh}}.
For the flesh craves what is contrary to the Spirit, and the Spirit what is contrary to the flesh. They are opposed to each other, so that you do not do what you want.
But if you are led by the Spirit, you are not under the law.

units.label: Works of the flesh, fruit of the Spirit
units.note: two harvests set side by side

verses.text: The acts of the flesh are obvious: sexual immorality, impurity, and debauchery;
idolatry and sorcery; hatred, discord, jealousy, and rage; rivalries, divisions, factions,
and envy; drunkenness, orgies, and the like. I warn you, as I did before, that those who practice such things will not inherit the kingdom of God.
But the {{karpos|fruit}} of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness,
gentleness, and {{egkrateia|self-control}}. Against such things there is no law.

units.label: Crucified flesh, keeping in step
units.note: live by the Spirit, walk by the Spirit

verses.text: Those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires.
Since we live by the Spirit, let us walk in step with the Spirit.
Let us not become conceited, provoking and envying one another.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, the Holy Spirit, the indwelling power of God in the believer. The whole passage answers how to live a holy life: by walking in the Spirit, being led by the Spirit, and keeping in step with the Spirit. Holiness is not self generated law keeping but the fruit the Spirit produces in those He indwells, so the Christian life is lived from the Spirit's power, not the flesh's effort.

sarx.gloss: Flesh, here not the physical body but the fallen human nature in rebellion against God, the self ruled by sinful desire. The flesh wars against the Spirit and produces its obvious works. Those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh, yet its desires still press, so the believer must keep walking by the Spirit rather than gratifying the flesh.

karpos.gloss: Fruit, the natural produce of a living plant. Paul calls the Spirit's work fruit, not works, because it grows organically from the Spirit's life within rather than being manufactured by effort. Notice it is singular, one fruit with many facets, love, joy, peace, and the rest, a single ripening character that the Spirit produces in the believer.

egkrateia.gloss: Self-control, mastery over one's desires and impulses. It stands last in the list of the Spirit's fruit, showing that true self control is itself a gift of the Spirit, not merely willpower. The Spirit who frees us from the law also produces the disciplined, ordered life that no law could compel, for against such things there is no law.

basic.context: Having freed the Galatians from the law, Paul answers the obvious question: how then do we live holy lives? His answer is the Spirit. Inside every believer the flesh and the Spirit are at war. If you walk by the Spirit, you will not carry out the flesh's desires. The flesh produces ugly, obvious works; the Spirit produces a beautiful, ripening character. Those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh, so they should keep in step with the Spirit.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the practical answer to the whole letter: freedom from law does not mean freedom to sin, because the Spirit produces holiness. Read the two lists as two harvests from two different roots, and notice that the answer to sin is not more law but more of the Spirit.
basic.originalLanguages: The command is to walk by the pneuma, the Spirit, and not gratify the sarx, the flesh, the fallen self. The Spirit produces karpos, fruit, a single ripening character, and it ends with egkrateia, self-control, itself a gift of the Spirit rather than mere willpower.
basic.typology: Crucifying the flesh continues the theme of being crucified with Christ from 2:20: the believer's union with the crucified Lord means death to the old self. The fruit of the Spirit is the restored image of God in humanity, the character of Christ formed in His people by the Spirit.
basic.christ: Those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh. The fruit of the Spirit is nothing less than the character of Christ reproduced in the believer, love, joy, peace, and the rest. The Spirit at work is the Spirit of Christ, forming the likeness of Jesus in those who are His.
basic.god: God gives the Spirit who wages war against the flesh and produces holiness. The kingdom of God is the inheritance from which the works of the flesh exclude, so God is both the giver of the Spirit and the King whose kingdom is at stake. He makes His people holy by His own indwelling power.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8 says those led by the Spirit put to death the deeds of the body. John 15 speaks of abiding in Christ to bear much fruit. Ephesians 5 contrasts the works of darkness with the fruit of light.
basic.oneStory: The gospel does not stop at forgiveness; it makes new people. The Spirit poured out in the last days, promised by the prophets, produces in believers the character God always intended, undoing the works of the flesh and forming the likeness of Christ, a preview of the holiness of the new creation.

context.literary: This section develops the ethics introduced in verse 13, that freedom is for love. It is built on the sharp contrast of two lists, the works of the flesh and the fruit of the Spirit, framed by commands to walk and keep in step with the Spirit. The singular fruit against the plural works is a deliberate and telling contrast.
context.historical: The agitators implied that without the law, Gentile believers would fall into moral chaos. Paul answers that the Spirit, not the law, is the true source of holiness. The works of the flesh listed include the very divisions, rivalries, and factions that the controversy was breeding among the Galatians.
context.canonical: The fruit of the Spirit is one of Scripture's best loved descriptions of Christian character, echoing the ethics of Jesus and the wider New Testament vision of life in the Spirit. It stands with Romans 8 as a foundational text on sanctification by the Spirit.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that holiness comes not through the law but through walking by the Spirit, to expose the works of the flesh and their exclusion from the kingdom, and to describe the fruit the Spirit produces in those who belong to Christ and have crucified the flesh.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The lists describe the two kinds of life and their outcomes. The commands, walk by the Spirit and keep in step with the Spirit, are prescriptive, and the warning that those who practice the works of the flesh will not inherit the kingdom is a sober statement of consequence.

debates.question: Does the warning in verse 21 threaten a believer's salvation?

views.label: A description of those who habitually practice the flesh
views.case: The phrase those who practice such things marks a settled way of life characteristic of the unregenerate; genuine believers, indwelt by the Spirit, are being changed and do not live in unbroken bondage to the flesh.

views.label: A serious call to persevere
views.case: The warning summons believers to take sin seriously and to walk by the Spirit, since a life wholly given to the flesh shows one does not belong to Christ.

debates.bearing: The verse does not teach salvation by works but warns that a life dominated by the flesh has no share in the kingdom; the Spirit's fruit is the evidence of belonging to Christ.

debates.question: How does one actually walk by the Spirit?

views.label: Active dependence and keeping in step
views.case: Walking is a deliberate, ongoing dependence on the Spirit, yielding to His leading and putting the flesh to death, not passive waiting; verse 25 calls it keeping in step.

views.label: Simply letting go and letting God
views.case: Some reduce it to passivity, but Paul commands active walking and speaks of believers crucifying the flesh, so it involves Spirit empowered effort.

debates.bearing: Holiness is neither self powered law keeping nor passive drifting; it is Spirit empowered, active dependence, walking day by day in step with the Spirit.

study.originalLanguages: The passage lives on a contrast of powers. The believer is to walk by the pneuma, the Spirit, the indwelling power of God, and so not gratify the sarx, the flesh, the fallen self in rebellion against God. These two are at war. From the flesh come obvious works, but from the Spirit comes karpos, fruit, and the word is singular: one ripening character with many facets, growing organically from the Spirit's life rather than manufactured by effort. The list climaxes in egkrateia, self-control, which shows that even mastery of our desires is the Spirit's gift, not mere willpower, for against such things there is no law.

typology.fulfillment: Continues the union with the crucified Christ of 2:20; the believer shares in Christ's death to sin and rises to new life in the Spirit.
Restores the image of God in humanity, the character of Christ formed in His people by the Spirit, foreshadowing the holiness of the new creation.

study.christ: Those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires, sharing in His death. And the fruit of the Spirit is nothing less than the character of Christ reproduced in the believer, for the love, joy, peace, and patience listed are the very traits displayed perfectly in Jesus. The Spirit who produces this fruit is the Spirit of Christ, and His work is to form the likeness of Jesus in those who are His, so that holiness is Christ taking shape within.
study.god: God gives the Holy Spirit who wars against the flesh and grows holiness in the believer, so that sanctification is God's own work within us. The kingdom of God is named as the inheritance forfeited by a life of the flesh, so God is both the gracious giver of the Spirit and the King whose kingdom is at stake. He does not leave His people to struggle alone under the law but dwells in them by His Spirit to make them like His Son.

crossReferences.note: By the Spirit put to death the deeds of the body; those led by the Spirit are sons of God.
Abiding in Christ the vine to bear much fruit, the source of the Spirit's fruit here.
The fruit of light in all goodness and righteousness and truth, against the works of darkness.
Putting to death the earthly nature and putting on love and the graces of Christ.

study.oneStory: The gospel does more than forgive; it remakes. The prophets promised that in the last days God would pour out His Spirit and give His people new hearts to walk in His ways. Here that promise is at work: the Spirit produces in believers the character God always intended, crucifying the works of the flesh and ripening the likeness of Christ. It is a foretaste of the perfect holiness of the new creation, where God's people will fully bear His image.

sermon.title: How to Change (Galatians 5:16 to 18, 22 to 25)
sermon.why: Keller expounds how real change comes through the fruit of the Spirit rather than mere willpower or law keeping, showing how walking by the Spirit produces the character of Christ in the believer.

quiz.q: How does Paul say we overcome the desires of the flesh?

options.text: By walking by the Spirit.
options.note: Verse 16 gives the Spirit, not the law, as the answer.

options.text: By trying harder to keep the law.
options.note: The answer to the flesh is the Spirit, not renewed law keeping.

options.text: By avoiding all contact with the world.
options.note: The battle is won by walking in the Spirit, not mere withdrawal.

options.text: By ignoring the flesh and hoping it fades.
options.note: Paul commands active walking by the Spirit, not passivity.

quiz.q: What is the relationship between the flesh and the Spirit?

options.text: They are opposed to each other, at war within the believer.
options.note: Verse 17 describes the ongoing conflict.

options.text: They work together in harmony.
options.note: They are opposed, not harmonious.

options.text: The flesh has already been completely removed.
options.note: The flesh is crucified yet its desires still press, so the war continues.

options.text: The Spirit rarely acts in a believer.
options.note: The Spirit actively wars against the flesh and produces fruit.

quiz.q: Why does Paul call the Spirit's produce fruit rather than works?

options.text: Because it grows organically from the Spirit's life within, not manufactured by effort.
options.note: The singular fruit grows from the Spirit, unlike self made works.

options.text: Because it is optional for believers.
options.note: It is the natural mark of the Spirit's presence, not optional.

options.text: Because it can be produced by willpower alone.
options.note: The point is that it is the Spirit's produce, not mere willpower.

options.text: Because it has nothing to do with character.
options.note: The fruit is precisely Christlike character formed by the Spirit.

quiz.q: What is true of those who belong to Christ Jesus?

options.text: They have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires.
options.note: Verse 24 describes their decisive break with the flesh.

options.text: They no longer experience any temptation.
options.note: The flesh is crucified, yet its desires still war, so temptation remains.

options.text: They are free to indulge the flesh.
options.note: Belonging to Christ means crucifying the flesh, not indulging it.

options.text: They rely on the law for holiness.
options.note: Holiness comes by the Spirit, not the law.

quiz.q: What does verse 25 call believers to do?

options.text: Since we live by the Spirit, to keep in step with the Spirit.
options.note: Life in the Spirit is to be matched by walking in the Spirit.

options.text: To relax, since the Spirit does everything without us.
options.note: Keeping in step is active, not passive.

options.text: To return to the law for daily guidance.
options.note: The call is to walk by the Spirit, not the law.

options.text: To compete with one another for status.
options.note: Verse 26 warns against conceit and provoking one another.

questions: Paul says the answer to the flesh is the Spirit, not the law. When you struggle with sin, do you reach first for willpower and rules, or for dependence on the Spirit?
The fruit of the Spirit is a single, ripening character. Which facet of that fruit is the Spirit most clearly growing in you right now, and which is least developed?
Those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh. What passion or desire do you need to treat as crucified rather than negotiated with?
Keeping in step with the Spirit is active, daily dependence. What would it look like practically to walk with the Spirit through tomorrow?
The works of the flesh include rivalries, divisions, and envy. How do these show up in your relationships, and how does the Spirit offer a different way?

translationNotes.note: Flesh renders sarx, here the fallen human nature in rebellion against God, not the physical body; the contrast is with the Spirit, not with material existence.
Fruit is singular in the Greek, one fruit with many facets, in deliberate contrast to the plural works of the flesh; the Spirit produces a unified character.
Against such things there is no law means the Spirit's fruit needs no law to restrain it and no law can produce it; it fulfills what the law aimed at.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 5:16-26. No em dashes in this passage required substitution. Sermon verified this session from the Gospel in Life series Galatians: New Freedom, New Family by Tim Keller.
